title: Superspace Coinvariants and Hyperplane Arrangements
overall_summary: The paper proves the Sagan-Swanson conjecture giving an explicit monomial basis for the type A superspace coinvariant ring SR = Ω/I, where Ω = K[x1,...,xn] ⊗ exterior(θ1,...,θn) and I is generated by positive-degree S_n-invariants in superspace. The central result is that, for each fermionic support J⊆[n], the bosonic monomials with exponents below the J-staircase form a basis of a commutative colon quotient S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), and therefore their products with θ_J form a basis of SR. The proof converts the colon quotients into Solomon-Terao algebras of hyperplane arrangements, proves freeness and exact deletion-restriction sequences for a class of southwest arrangements, and inducts to obtain monomial bases. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most useful output is that every bigraded component of SR is counted explicitly by pairs consisting of a subset J and an exponent vector a with 0≤a_i<st(J)_i; once hook Schur coefficients are related to superspace bidegrees, this turns them into a direct combinatorial counting problem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, Rhoades-Wilson transfer principle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a characteristic-zero field, S=K[x1,...,xn], Ω=S⊗exterior(θ1,...,θn), and SR=Ω/I be the superspace coinvariant ring, where I is the ideal generated by S_n-invariants in Ω with vanishing constant term. For J⊆[n], define θ_J=θ_{j1}...θ_{jr} for J={j1<...<jr} and define f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i) in S. If, for every J⊆[n], B(J)⊆S is a set of homogeneous polynomials whose images form a K-basis of S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), then the disjoint union B=⊔_{J⊆[n]} B(J) θ_J has images forming a K-basis of SR.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Rhoades-Wilson and uses it as a black box. Its role is to decompose the superspace quotient by fermionic support J: the bosonic relations seen in the θ_J-sector are encoded by the colon ideal (S^{S_n}_+):f_J. Therefore, once one supplies a basis for each commutative quotient S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), multiplying those basis elements by θ_J and taking the disjoint union gives a basis of the whole superspace coinvariant ring.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Rhoades-Wilson colon-ideal dichotomy quoted in the introduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], let st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) be defined recursively by st(J)_1=1 if 1∉J and st(J)_1=0 if 1∈J, and for i>1 by st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1}+1 if i∉J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1} if i∈J. With f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i), Rhoades-Wilson proved that (S^{S_n}_+):f_J is the unit ideal S if 1∈J, while if 1∉J it is generated by a homogeneous regular sequence p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} with deg(p_{J,i})=st(J)_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated in the introduction as a prior result from Rhoades-Wilson and is not reproved in this paper. The paper uses the dichotomy mainly for dimension and Hilbert-series control: in the nonunit case the quotient S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) is a complete intersection with Hilbert series ∏_{i=1}^n (1−q^{st(J)_i})/(1−q), while in the unit case the quotient is zero, matching the fact that the staircase monomial set M(J) is empty when 1∈J.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1, Poincare-duality colon criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a and a′ be homogeneous ideals in S=K[x1,...,xn], and let f∈S be homogeneous of degree k with f∉a. Suppose a′⊆(a:f). If S/a′ is a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree m and S/a is a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree m+k, then a′=(a:f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this lemma from Abe-Horiguchi-Masuda-Murai-Sato. The mechanism is that the inclusion a′⊆(a:f) gives a quotient map S/a′→S/(a:f), while multiplication by f embeds S/(a:f), shifted by degree k, into S/a. The assumed socle degrees differ by exactly k, and Poincare duality forces the annihilator of f in S/a to have the same size as S/a′; hence the quotient map cannot have a nonzero kernel and the two ideals coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3, trichotomy for Solomon-Terao algebras of free arrangements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a free hyperplane arrangement in K^n with exponent multiset {e1,...,en}. Let Der(A) be its module of logarithmic derivations, let c:Der(S)→S be an S-module map homogeneous of degree d≥0, meaning each c(∂_i) is homogeneous of degree d, and define cA=c(Der(A)) and ST(A,c)=S/cA. Exactly one of the following holds: ST(A,c)=0; ST(A,c) is infinite-dimensional over K; or cA is a complete intersection and ST(A,c) is a Poincare duality algebra with Hilbert series ∏_{i=1}^n (1−q^{e_i+d})/(1−q)^n and socle degree e1+...+en+n(d−1)=|A|+n(d−1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a homogeneous free S-basis ρ1,...,ρn of Der(A) with deg ρ_i=e_i. Then cA is generated by the n homogeneous elements c(ρ_i), whose degrees are e_i+d. If S/cA is neither zero nor infinite-dimensional, then cA is a proper Artinian ideal generated by n homogeneous elements in a polynomial ring of dimension n, hence these generators form a regular sequence. Standard complete-intersection theory gives the Hilbert series and Poincare duality. The equality e1+...+en=|A| is the usual consequence of Terao factorization for free arrangements, obtained by comparing the coefficient of t^{n−1} in the characteristic polynomial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4, Solomon-Terao ideals as colon ideals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B⊆A be free hyperplane arrangements in K^n, let Q(A) and Q(B) be defining products of linear forms for A and B, and let c:Der(S)→S be homogeneous. Assume ST(A,c)=S/cA is finite-dimensional over K and Q(A)/Q(B)∉cA. Then cB=cA:(Q(A)/Q(B)) as ideals of S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since B⊆A, every derivation tangent to A is tangent to B, and multiplication by Q(A)/Q(B) sends Der(B) into Der(A). Applying c gives cB⊆cA:(Q(A)/Q(B)). The assumptions imply that ST(A,c) is a nonzero finite-dimensional Poincare duality algebra by Lemma 3.3. Because cA⊆cB, the quotient ST(B,c) is also finite-dimensional; the preceding inclusion and the hypothesis Q(A)/Q(B)∉cA ensure it is nonzero, so Lemma 3.3 again gives Poincare duality. The socle degrees of ST(A,c) and ST(B,c) differ by deg(Q(A)/Q(B))=|A|−|B|, exactly the hypothesis needed in Lemma 2.1, which yields equality of the colon ideal with cB.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2, deletion and restriction for southwest arrangements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let AeΦ+ be the augmented braid arrangement in K^n with hyperplanes H_{i,j} for 0≤i<j≤n, where H_{0,j} is the coordinate hyperplane x_j=0 and H_{i,j} for i>0 is x_i=x_j. A subarrangement A⊆AeΦ+ is southwest if H_{i,j}∈A and j>i+1 imply H_{i,j−1}∈A. Define h(A)=(h1,...,hn) by h_i=|A∩{H_{0,i},H_{1,i},...,H_{i−1,i}}|. If A is southwest, contains at least one coordinate hyperplane, and p=max{k:H_{0,k}∈A}, then A\H_{0,p} is southwest with h-sequence (h1,...,h_{p−1},h_p−1,h_{p+1},...,hn), and the restriction A(p)=A^{H_{0,p}} is southwest inside H_{0,p} with h-sequence (h1,...,h_{p−1},h_{p+1},...,hn).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Deleting the coordinate hyperplane H_{0,p} simply removes one hyperplane from the p-th northwest-to-southeast diagonal, so the southwest condition and all other h_i are unchanged. For restriction to H_{0,p}, one sets x_p=0. Hyperplanes not involving x_p remain of the same form, while hyperplanes x_i−x_p=0 become coordinate hyperplanes x_i=0 in the restricted space; pictorially, the hyperplanes involving x_p form a V-shape in the augmented root poset, and restriction removes one arm and moves the other arm to the top of the corresponding diagonals. The maximality of p among coordinate hyperplanes in A ensures this process preserves southwest-closedness and has precisely the stated effect on h-sequences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3, southwest arrangements are free
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every southwest subarrangement A⊆AeΦ+ is supersolvable, and hence free.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Replace each coordinate hyperplane x_k=0 by x_0−x_k=0 to obtain a graphical arrangement A′ in K^{n+1} associated with a graph Γ on vertices {0,1,...,n}. The intersection posets of A and A′ are isomorphic, so supersolvability is the same for the two arrangements. The southwest condition implies that Γ is chordal. Stanley's criterion says that a graphical arrangement is supersolvable if and only if its graph is chordal. Since supersolvable arrangements are free, A is free.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.6, explicit free basis for derivations of a southwest arrangement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ be a southwest arrangement in K^n. For 0≤i<k≤n set α_{0,k}=x_k and α_{i,k}=x_i−x_k for i>0. For j=1,...,n define ρ_j^A=Σ_{k=j}^n (∏_{0≤i<j, H_{i,j}∈A} α_{i,k}) ∂_k, with the empty product equal to 1. Then ρ_1^A,...,ρ_n^A form a homogeneous free S-basis of Der(A). In particular, the exponent multiset of A is h(A)=(h1,...,hn), where h_j=|A∩{H_{0,j},H_{1,j},...,H_{j−1,j}}|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof applies Saito's criterion. The degree of ρ_j^A is h_j, so the sum of the degrees is |A|. The derivations are S-linearly independent because ρ_j^A only involves ∂_j,∂_{j+1},...,∂_n and the coefficient of ∂_j is nonzero, giving a triangular form. It remains to show ρ_j^A is tangent to every hyperplane H_{s,t} in A. If t<j, then ρ_j^A(α_{s,t})=0. If s<j≤t, southwest-closedness implies H_{s,j}∈A, so the factor α_{s,t} appears in the relevant product and divides ρ_j^A(α_{s,t}). If j≤s, then ρ_j^A(α_{s,t}) is a difference of two products that becomes zero after setting x_s=x_t, hence is divisible by x_s−x_t. Thus every ρ_j^A lies in Der(A), and Saito's criterion gives the basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9 and Lemma 4.10, exact derivation sequence for southwest deletion-restriction triples
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a hyperplane arrangement in K^n and let H=ker(α)∈A. Suppose there are nonnegative integers e1≤...≤en and an index k such that A is free with exponent multiset {e1,...,e_{k−1},e_k,e_{k+1},...,en}, A\H is free with exponent multiset {e1,...,e_{k−1},e_k−1,e_{k+1},...,en}, and A^H is free with exponent multiset {e1,...,e_{k−1},e_{k+1},...,en}. Then there is a short exact sequence 0→Der(A\H) --multiply by α--> Der(A) --restriction to H--> Der(A^H)→0. Moreover, if A⊆AeΦ+ is an essential southwest arrangement and p=max{k:H_{0,k}∈A}, then the triple (A, A\H_{0,p}, A^{H_{0,p}}) satisfies these freeness and exponent hypotheses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the exact sequence, a derivation tangent to A restricts to a derivation tangent to the restricted arrangement A^H on H; the kernel consists of derivations divisible by α, which identify with Der(A\H). Surjectivity is the content of the addition-deletion theorem under the stated exponent hypotheses. For an essential southwest arrangement, Proposition 4.3 gives freeness, Proposition 4.6 identifies the exponents with h(A), and Lemma 4.2 shows that deletion of H_{0,p} subtracts 1 from h_p while restriction deletes h_p. Thus the exponents match Terao's addition-deletion pattern.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1, cospanning criterion for products of augmented positive roots
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V=K^n with dual basis x1,...,xn, and let eΦ+={x_i−x_j:1≤i<j≤n}∪{x1,...,xn}. For any subset T⊆eΦ+, the product ∏_{α∈T} α lies in the classical coinvariant ideal (S^{S_n}_+) if and only if the complement eΦ+\T does not span V* over K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is in the appendix. First suppose T^c=eΦ+\T does not span V*. Let B={b:x_b∈span_K(T^c)} and A=[n]\B, so A is nonempty. Then x_a∈T for a∈A and, up to sign, x_a−x_b∈T for a∈A,b∈B; hence it is enough to show P=∏_{a∈A} x_a ∏_{a∈A,b∈B}(x_a−x_b) lies in (S^{S_n}_+). Expanding in elementary symmetric polynomials in the B-variables and using the congruence h_d(A)≡(−1)^d e_d(B) modulo (S^{S_n}_+) rewrites P modulo the coinvariant ideal in terms of complete homogeneous functions in the A-variables. Repeatedly, Pieri-rule identities show the terms to be Schur polynomials s_λ(A) with λ_1>|B|=n−|A|, hence in (S^{S_n}_+); eventually P is congruent to the rectangular Schur polynomial s_{(|B|+1)^{|A|}}(A), also in the ideal. Conversely, suppose T^c spans V*. Build a forest F on [n] whose edges correspond to differences in T^c and such that each component has a distinguished root i_p with x_{i_p}∈T^c. Then ∏_{α∈T}α divides a polynomial P_F formed from x_i over nonroot vertices and x_i−x_j over nonedges of F. If ∏_{α∈T}α were in the coinvariant ideal then P_F would be also. By Steinberg's inverse-system criterion, membership in (S^{S_n}_+) is equivalent to annihilating the Vandermonde δ_n under differential action. Comparing P_F with the full Vandermonde product, monomials are interpreted as orientations of the complete graph; orient the forest away from roots and then repeatedly choose leaves, orienting all remaining incident edges toward the chosen leaf. This produces a unique Vandermonde-type monomial x_{w(1)}^{n−1}x_{w(2)}^{n−2}...x_{w(n)}^0 appearing without cancellation in P_F, so P_F⊙δ_n≠0. Hence P_F and therefore ∏_{α∈T}α are not forced into the coinvariant ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2, Solomon-Terao algebras of subarrangements of the augmented braid arrangement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ be any subarrangement, where AeΦ+ consists of H_{i,j}=ker(α_{i,j}) for 0≤i<j≤n with α_{0,j}=x_j and α_{i,j}=x_i−x_j for i>0. Let i:Der(S)→S be the S-module map i(∂_r)=1 for all r, let iA=i(Der(A)), define ST(A,i)=S/iA, and set β_A=∏_{0≤i<j≤n, H_{i,j}∉A} α_{i,j}. Then: 1. ST(A,i) is a quotient of the classical coinvariant ring S/(S^{S_n}_+), equivalently iA contains (S^{S_n}_+). 2. If A is not essential, meaning ∩_{H∈A}H≠{0}, then iA=S and ST(A,i)=0. 3. If A is free, then iA=(S^{S_n}_+):β_A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part 1, the explicit free bases for the braid arrangement AΦ+ and augmented braid arrangement AeΦ+ imply i(Der(AeΦ+))=a(Der(AΦ+))=(S^{S_n}_+), where a:Der(S)→S sends ∂_r to x_r. Since A⊆AeΦ+ gives Der(AeΦ+)⊆Der(A), iA contains the coinvariant ideal. For part 2, after harmless closure reductions, let T={r:H_{0,r}∈A}. Nonessentiality implies T≠[n]. On [n]\T, put i∼j when H_{i,j}∈A. If C1,...,Cr are the equivalence classes, then the constant derivation ∑_{u∈C1∪...∪Cr} ∂_u is tangent to every hyperplane of A, and applying i gives a nonzero scalar, hence a unit. For part 3, if A is nonessential this follows from part 2 and Lemma 5.1, since β_A∈(S^{S_n}_+). If A is essential and free, then Der(A) has no nonzero degree-zero elements, so iA has no nonzero constants; Lemma 5.1 gives β_A∉(S^{S_n}_+). Applying Lemma 3.4 to the inclusion A⊆AeΦ+ and using i(Der(AeΦ+))=(S^{S_n}_+) gives iA=(S^{S_n}_+):β_A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3, characteristic polynomial and freeness of A_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], define the arrangement A_J⊆AeΦ+ by A_J={H_{x_j−x_i}: j∉J and i>j} ∪ {H_{x_j}: j∉J}. With st(J)_i as in the J-staircase recursion, the characteristic polynomial of A_J is χ_{A_J}(t)=∏_{i=1}^n (t−st(J)_i). In particular, A_J is supersolvable and free with exponents st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The characteristic polynomial is computed by the finite-fields method. For a sufficiently large prime p, χ_{A_J}(p) equals the number of points (a1,...,an)∈(Z/pZ)^n avoiding all hyperplanes of A_J. Choose the coordinates sequentially. At step i, the forbidden values are 0 if i∉J, together with the previously chosen a_j for j<i and j∉J. The number of forbidden values is exactly st(J)_i, so the number of choices is p−st(J)_i and the total count is ∏_i(p−st(J)_i). Supersolvability can be checked from the associated chordal graph, and freeness follows from supersolvability; the roots of the characteristic polynomial match the exponents.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4, colon quotients as Solomon-Terao algebras for A_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every J⊆[n], with f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i) and A_J={H_{x_j−x_i}: j∉J and i>j} ∪ {H_{x_j}: j∉J}, one has ST(A_J,i)=S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), where i:Der(S)→S sends every ∂_r to 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The missing hyperplanes of A_J inside AeΦ+ are exactly the coordinate hyperplanes H_{x_j} with j∈J and the difference hyperplanes H_{x_j−x_i} with j∈J and i>j. Therefore β_{A_J}=∏_{H∉A_J} α_H equals f_J up to the fixed sign convention. Lemma 5.3 gives that A_J is free, so Proposition 5.2(3) applies and gives iA_J=(S^{S_n}_+):f_J. Taking quotients yields the stated equality of algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6, explicit regular sequence generating (S^{S_n}_+):f_J
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], a homogeneous free basis ρ_1^J,...,ρ_n^J of Der(A_J) is given as follows. If i∉J, then ρ_i^J=Σ_{k=i}^n (∏_{j∉J, j<i} (x_j−x_k)) x_k ∂_k. If i∈J, then ρ_i^J=(∏_{j∉J, j<i} (x_j−x_i)) ∂_i. Define g_{J,i}=i(ρ_i^J), where i(∂_k)=1; explicitly, if i∉J then g_{J,i}=Σ_{k=i}^n (∏_{j∉J, j<i}(x_j−x_k))x_k, while if i∈J then g_{J,i}=∏_{j∉J, j<i}(x_j−x_i). If 1∈J then (S^{S_n}_+):f_J=S. If 1∉J then (S^{S_n}_+):f_J is generated by the regular sequence g_{J,1},...,g_{J,n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the derivation basis, the proof again uses Saito's criterion. The degree of ρ_i^J is st(J)_i, so the degrees sum to |A_J| by Lemma 5.3. The derivations are triangular in the partial derivatives because ρ_i^J only involves ∂_i,...,∂_n and has nonzero ∂_i coefficient. Divisibility by the defining forms of A_J is checked case by case: for coordinate forms x_j with j∉J, ρ_i^J(x_j) is either zero or visibly divisible by x_j; for difference forms x_j−x_l with j∉J and j<l, the image is either a difference of two products that vanishes when x_j=x_l, or contains x_j−x_l as a displayed factor, or is zero. Thus ρ_i^J∈Der(A_J). Applying the map i to this free basis gives generators of iA_J. Theorem 5.4 identifies iA_J with the colon ideal. If 1∉J the degrees are positive and the quotient is finite-dimensional, so the generators form a regular sequence; if 1∈J the colon ideal is the unit ideal by the Rhoades-Wilson dichotomy, equivalently by Lemma 5.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1, short exact sequence of Solomon-Terao algebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ and H=ker(α)∈A. Assume A, A\H, and A^H satisfy the addition-deletion freeness and exponent hypotheses: their exponent multisets differ by replacing one exponent e_k of A by e_k−1 for A\H and deleting e_k for A^H. Then, for i:Der(S)→S with i(∂_r)=1, the restricted Solomon-Terao algebra satisfies ST(A^H,i)=S/(iA+(α)), under the natural identification of the coordinate ring of H with S/(α). Moreover there is a short exact sequence 0→ST(A\H,i) --multiply by α--> ST(A,i) → ST(A^H,i)→0, where the last map is induced by the quotient S→S/(α).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The restriction map η_H:Der(A)→Der(A^H) from the derivation exact sequence is compatible with the quotient map π:S→S/(α) and the maps i, giving a commutative diagram Der(A)→Der(A^H) and S→S/(α). Hence i(Der(A^H))=(iA+(α))/(α), which proves ST(A^H,i)=S/(iA+(α)). The standard colon exact sequence gives 0→S/(iA:α) --multiply by α--> S/iA → S/(iA+(α))→0. Lemma 3.4 identifies iA:α with i(A\H), so the left term is ST(A\H,i), yielding the stated short exact sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.2, monomial basis for Solomon-Terao algebras of essential southwest arrangements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A⊆AeΦ+ be an essential southwest arrangement in K^n, and let h(A)=(h1,...,hn) with h_i=|A∩{H_{0,i},H_{1,i},...,H_{i−1,i}}|. Then the monomials {x1^{a1}...xn^{an}: 0≤a_i<h_i for all i} descend to a K-basis of ST(A,i)=S/i(Der(A)), where i(∂_r)=1 for every r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a double induction on n and |A|. The cases n=1 and |A|=n are immediate: for |A|=n essentiality forces a linear complete intersection, so the quotient is K and h_i=1 for all i. In the inductive step, choose p=max{k:H_{0,k}∈A}. By Lemma 4.10 and Lemma 6.1 there is an exact sequence 0→ST(A\H_{0,p},i) --multiply by x_p--> ST(A,i) → ST(A^{H_{0,p}},i)→0. Lemma 4.2 identifies the h-sequences of the deletion and restriction. If h_p>1, the deletion has h_p decreased by 1 and the restriction has the p-th entry removed; by induction, monomials in ST(A,i) are exactly the union of monomials not involving x_p coming from the restriction and x_p times monomials from the deletion, giving exponents 0≤a_p<h_p. If h_p=1, the deletion is nonessential, so Proposition 5.2 gives ST(A\H_{0,p},i)=0; hence ST(A,i)≅ST(A^{H_{0,p}},i) via setting x_p=0, and the allowed monomials are precisely those with a_p=0. This proves both spanning and linear independence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1, monomial basis for the colon quotients S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For J⊆[n], define the J-staircase st(J) by st(J)_1=1 if 1∉J and 0 if 1∈J, and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1}+1 for i>1 with i∉J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1} for i>1 with i∈J. Let M(J)={x1^{a1}...xn^{an}: 0≤a_i<st(J)_i for all i}. Then M(J) descends to a K-basis of S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J), where f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j−x_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The quotient dimension agrees with |M(J)| by the Rhoades-Wilson Hilbert-series result, equivalently by the complete-intersection description in Corollary 5.6, so it suffices to prove linear independence. Define the modified product f̃_J=∏_{j∈J}∏_{i=j+1}^n(x_j−x_i), omitting the coordinate factors x_j. Let A= {H_{j,i}: j∉J, i=j+1,...,n} ∪ {H_{x1},...,H_{xn}} = A_J ∪ {H_{x_k}:k∈J}. This A is an essential southwest arrangement, and β_A=f̃_J, so Proposition 5.2 gives S/((S^{S_n}_+):f̃_J)=ST(A,i). Multiplication by ∏_{j∈J}x_j gives an injection S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) → S/((S^{S_n}_+):f̃_J). The h-sequence of A satisfies h_k=st(J)_k for k∉J and h_k=st(J)_k+1 for k∈J. By Theorem 6.2, monomials with exponents b_k<st(J)_k for k∉J and b_k<st(J)_k+1 for k∈J form a basis of the target. Multiplying an element x^a∈M(J) by ∏_{j∈J}x_j shifts exactly the exponents indexed by J up by 1, producing distinct target basis monomials; hence the original M(J) is linearly independent in the source.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 7.2, Sagan-Swanson superspace Artin basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Ω=K[x1,...,xn]⊗exterior(θ1,...,θn), let SR=Ω/I be the superspace coinvariant ring, and for J⊆[n] let θ_J be the product of θ_j over j∈J in increasing order. With M(J) as in Theorem 7.1, the set M=⊔_{J⊆[n]} M(J) θ_J descends to a K-basis of SR.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the Rhoades-Wilson transfer principle, Theorem 1.2, using the bases B(J)=M(J) supplied by Theorem 7.1 for all commutative colon quotients S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J). The resulting disjoint union over fermionic supports J is exactly the Sagan-Swanson set of superspace Artin monomials.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1, auxiliary facts about the classical coinvariant ideal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let δ_n=∏_{1≤i<j≤n}(x_i−x_j) be the Vandermonde determinant and let the differential action of S on itself be f⊙g=f(∂_1,...,∂_n)g. Then: 1. f∈(S^{S_n}_+) if and only if f⊙δ_n=0. 2. If A⊆[n] and λ is a partition with λ_1>n−|A|, then the Schur polynomial s_λ(A), obtained by setting variables outside A to zero, lies in (S^{S_n}_+). 3. If [n]=A⊔B, then h_d(A)≡(−1)^d e_d(B) modulo (S^{S_n}_+) for every d≥0, where h_d is complete homogeneous and e_d is elementary symmetric.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Part 1 is Steinberg's inverse-system description of the coinvariant ideal. For part 2, first handle one-row partitions using h_d(A)=h_d(A∪{a})−x_a h_{d−1}(A∪{a}) and h_d([n])∈(S^{S_n}_+) for d>0, inducting downward on n−|A|; then use the Jacobi-Trudi determinant for general λ and expand along the first row. For part 3, work in the coinvariant ring R=S/(S^{S_n}_+) and adjoin a variable t. Since ∏_{i=1}^n(1−x_i t)=1 in R[t] and each 1−x_i t is invertible, multiplying by ∏_{a∈A}(1−x_a t)^{−1} gives ∏_{b∈B}(1−x_b t)=∏_{a∈A}(1−x_a t)^{−1}; comparing coefficients of t^d gives the congruence.

proof_techniques.name: Transfer from superspace to commutative colon quotients
proof_techniques.description: The paper avoids direct Grobner or straightening analysis in the supercommutative ring Ω by using the Rhoades-Wilson transfer principle. The θ-support J of a superspace monomial determines a polynomial f_J, and the bosonic part of the quotient in that θ-sector is controlled by the colon ideal (S^{S_n}_+):f_J. Thus the problem of producing a basis of SR is reduced to producing compatible bases of ordinary commutative quotients of S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients, one can first pass through the superspace model and then count basis elements by fermionic degree and bosonic degree. This bypasses the need to manipulate the full multigraded m-set coinvariant algebra directly.

proof_techniques.name: Model colon ideals as Solomon-Terao ideals
proof_techniques.description: For a subarrangement A of the augmented braid arrangement AeΦ+, the paper studies the Solomon-Terao ideal iA=i(Der(A)), where i sends every partial derivative ∂_r to 1. Proposition 5.2 proves that, when A is free, iA is the colon ideal (S^{S_n}_+):β_A, where β_A is the product of the linear forms corresponding to hyperplanes missing from A. This converts algebraic colon-ideal questions into freeness and derivation-basis questions for arrangements.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The hook-relevant quotients S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) are exactly ST(A_J,i). This gives both their Hilbert series and explicit generators, and provides a geometric route to monomial bases.

proof_techniques.name: Southwest arrangements and deletion-restriction induction
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces southwest subarrangements of AeΦ+, whose defining hyperplanes are closed under moving southwest in the augmented type A root poset. These arrangements are supersolvable and free, have explicit h-sequences, and are closed under deleting the maximal coordinate hyperplane and restricting to it. The deletion and restriction h-sequences differ in exactly the way needed for an induction on monomial exponent bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The final proof of the M(J) basis embeds the quotient of interest into a quotient for a southwest arrangement. The recursive structure explains why the J-staircase bounds are the correct combinatorial constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Exact sequences of Solomon-Terao algebras
proof_techniques.description: From Terao addition-deletion, the paper obtains a short exact sequence of derivation modules and then pushes it through the map i:Der(S)→S. This yields 0→ST(A\H,i) --×α--> ST(A,i) → ST(A^H,i)→0. The sequence separates monomials in ST(A,i) into those divisible by α and those surviving after setting α=0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For α=x_p, this exact sequence gives a clean induction proving that exponent bounds are 0≤a_p<h_p. Similar exact sequences could be used to prove other monomial-counting interpretations without constructing a Grobner basis.

proof_techniques.name: Triangular derivation bases via Saito's criterion
proof_techniques.description: For southwest arrangements and for A_J, the paper writes down derivations ρ_i whose coefficient matrix is triangular with nonzero diagonal entries. Saito's criterion reduces freeness to checking that each ρ_i sends every defining linear form into its own principal ideal and that the sum of degrees is the number of hyperplanes. The divisibility checks are elementary case analyses using the southwest condition or the defining pattern of A_J.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The explicit derivations yield the concrete regular sequence g_{J,1},...,g_{J,n} generating the hook-sector colon ideals. This can be used if a solver wants an algebra presentation in addition to a counting basis.

proof_techniques.name: Poincare-duality colon comparison
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.4 identifies one Solomon-Terao ideal as a colon of another by combining two facts: multiplication by a missing product of hyperplane forms sends Der(B) into Der(A), and the corresponding quotients are Poincare duality complete intersections with socle degrees differing by the degree of that missing product. The Poincare-duality colon criterion then forces the inclusion of ideals to be equality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the technical bridge proving that arrangement-generated ideals are exactly the desired coinvariant colon ideals, not merely subideals. It is useful whenever the hook-sector quotient is approached through a larger complete intersection.

proof_techniques.name: Cospanning criterion for products of roots
proof_techniques.description: The appendix proves that a product of augmented positive roots lies in the classical coinvariant ideal exactly when the omitted roots fail to span the dual space. The proof combines Schur-function congruences in partial variable sets for one direction and Steinberg's Vandermonde inverse-system criterion plus a forest-orientation construction for the other direction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This criterion verifies nonvanishing of colon multipliers such as β_A and f_J modulo the coinvariant ideal. It helps justify injections and exclude degenerate cases in hook-sector computations.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-fields method for degrees and exponents
proof_techniques.description: For A_J, the characteristic polynomial is computed by counting points over a large finite field outside the arrangement. The coordinates are chosen in order; the number of forbidden values at step i is exactly st(J)_i. This gives χ_{A_J}(t)=∏_i(t−st(J)_i), matching the degrees of the regular sequence and the staircase Hilbert product.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides an independent combinatorial explanation of the factors ∏[st(J)_i]_q appearing in the hook-sector Hilbert series. It is a useful sanity check for any proposed hook coefficient formula.

proof_techniques.name: Remove coordinate factors, prove independence in a larger southwest quotient, then inject back
proof_techniques.description: The key move in Theorem 7.1 is to replace f_J by f̃_J, omitting the coordinate factors x_j for j∈J. The corresponding arrangement is southwest, so Theorem 6.2 gives a monomial basis for S/((S^{S_n}_+):f̃_J) with one extra allowed exponent in positions j∈J. Multiplication by ∏_{j∈J}x_j injects S/((S^{S_n}_+):f_J) into this larger quotient and sends the proposed M(J) monomials to distinct basis monomials.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This directly explains the combinatorics of the J-staircase basis: positions in J are shifted once by the injection, while all positions retain their original staircase bounds in the source quotient.

other_useful_info: Immediate consequence of Corollary 7.2: the bigraded Hilbert series of SR with q marking total x-degree and z marking θ-degree is counted by Hilb(SR;q,z)=Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q, where [r]_q=1+q+...+q^{r−1} for r>0 and [0]_q=0. Equivalently, the coefficient of q^d z^r is the number of pairs (J,(a1,...,an)) with J⊆[n], |J|=r, 0≤a_i<st(J)_i, and Σ_i a_i=d. The paper emphasizes that its transfer principle is non-equivariant; it proves Hilbert-series and basis statements, but not the conjectural Sn-isotypic basis of Kelvin Chan. Section 8 points to possible extensions to other reflection types and notes that analogous superspace coinvariant bases in type B remain conjectural in related work of Sagan-Swanson.
why_relevant: Gives the first explicit monomial basis for the superspace coinvariant ring, proving the Sagan-Swanson basis conjecture. This basis turns the Hilbert-series data needed for hook coefficients into an explicit combinatorial counting problem.
